---
title: "Overview of Quasi-Experimental Designs"
subtitle: "Advanced Social Epidemiology PhD Course <br> University of Copenhagen </br>"
author: "Sam Harper"
date: 2026-06-11
date-format: iso
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../custom.scss]
    width: 1600
    height: 900
    slide-number: true
    html-math-method: mathjax
    embed-resources: true
    auto-stretch: false
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        /* deck-wide body text size (headers unchanged) */
        .reveal .slides section p,
        .reveal .slides section li,
        .reveal .slides section td,
        .reveal .slides section th {
          font-size: 38px;
        }
        .reveal .slides section .footnote p {
          font-size: inherit;
        }
        </style>
title-slide-attributes:
    data-background-image: "../../images/mcgill-epi-logo.png"
    data-background-size: 30%
    data-background-position: 50% 90%
editor: source
bibliography: references.bib
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(here)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center",
  out.width = "100%"
)
```

## Overview {.h2center}

### [Motivation]{.black}

<br>

### [Quasi-Experimental Designs]{.black}

<br>

### [Final Thoughts]{.black}

## Overview {.h2center}

### [Motivation]{.black}

<br>

### [Quasi-Experimental Designs]{.gray}

<br>

### [Final Thoughts]{.gray}

## What's the problem?

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}

<br>

-   We are mainly (though not exclusively) interested in causal effects.

-   We want to know:

    -   Should we intervene to reduce exposure to $X$?, or
    -   Did the program work? If so, for whom? If not, why not?, or
    -   If we implement the program elsewhere, should we expect the same result?

-   These questions involve counterfactuals about what would happen **if** we intervened to do something.

-   These are causal questions.
:::
:::::

## Causation, Association, and Confounding

-   **Causal effect**: Difference between those randomly
    [assigned]{style="color: red"} to treatment:

    $$E\left(Y|SET\left[Treated\right]\right)-E\left(Y|SET\left[Untreated\right]\right)$$

-   **Association**: Difference between those who [happen to be]{style="color: red"} treated:

    $$E\left(Y|Treated\right)-E(Y|Untreated)$$

-   **Confounding**:
   $$
\begin{gathered}
E\left(Y|SET\left[Treated\right]\right)-E\left(Y|SET\left[Untreated\right]\right) \\
\neq \\
E\left(Y|Treated\right)-E\left(Y|Untreated\right)
\end{gathered}
$$


##

### Strength of randomized treatment allocation

-   Recall that randomization means that we can generally estimate the causal effect *of being randomized* without bias.

-   Randomization guarantees exchangeability on measured and unmeasured factors.

```{r, out.width="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "rct-dag.png"))
```

##

::: footnote
@keall2015
:::

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Randomize if you can

-   Randomization leads to:

    -   balance on measured factors.
    -   balance on unmeasured factors.

-   Unmeasured factors cannot bias the estimate of the exposure effect.

-   Example from Home Injury Prevention Intervention cluster RCT

-   What do you notice about Table 1?
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r, out.width="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "keall-lancet-t1.png"))
```
:::
:::::

## Or maybe don't randomize?

:::: columns
::: {.column width="45%"}
<br>

### RCT limitations

-   Non-compliance.
-   Attrition.
-   Spillovers.
-   Blinding (esp. in clinical trials).
:::

::: {.column width = "5%"}
::::

::: {.column width="50%"}
<br>

### Other trial challenges:

-   Unethical (poverty, parental social class, job loss)
-   Impossible (ethnic background, place of birth)
-   Expensive (neighborhood environments)
-   Long latency periods (many years before outcomes are observable).

-   We need alternatives to RCTs.
:::

:::


## 

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Key issue is credibility

-   If we have a good design and assume that we have measured all of the confounders, then regression can give us exactly what we want: an estimate of the causal effect of exposure to $T$.

-   Core issue: How credible is this assumption?
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r, out.width="90%"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "wishful-thinking-veley.jpg"))
```
:::
:::::

##

::: footnote
@jones2019
:::

### Why we worry about observational studies

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
Recent evaluation of "Workplace Wellness" program in US state of Illinois

Treatment: biometric health screening; online health risk assessment, access to a wide variety of wellness activities (e.g., smoking cessation, stress management, and recreational classes).

Randomized evaluation:

-   3,300 individuals assigned treated group.
-   1,534 assigned to control (could not access the program).

Also analyzed as an observational study comparing "participants" vs. non-participants in treated group.
:::
:::::

##

::: footnote
@carroll2018
:::

```{r, out.width="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "carroll-nyt-wellness.png"))
```



## How can quasi-experiments help?

<br>

### Quasi-experiments aim to mimic RCTs.

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}


-   "Accidents of chance" that create:

    1.  Comparable treated and control units
    2.  Random or "as-if" random assignment to treatment.

-   Control for (some) sources of bias that cannot be adequately controlled using regression adjustment.

-   More credible designs also help us to understand the relevance of other factors that may be implicated in generating inequalities.
:::
:::::

## Selection on "observables" and "unobservables"

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
<br>

-   Observables: Things you measured or can measure

-   Unobservables: Things you can't measure (e.g., innate abilities, motivation)

-   Exogenous variation: predicts exposure but (**we assume**) [not]{.red} associated with anything else \[mimicking random assignment\].
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
<br>

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "qe-dag.png"))
```
:::
:::::

##

### Strategies based on observables and unobservables

:::: columns
::: {.column width="45%"}
<br>

-   Most observational study designs control for *measured* factors using:

    -   Stratification
    -   Regression adjustment
    -   Matching (propensity scores, etc.)
:::

::: {.column width = "5%"}
::::

::: {.column width="45%"}
<br>

-   Quasi-experimental strategies [aim]{.red} to control for some *unmeasured* factors using:

    -   Interrupted time series (ITS)
    -   Difference-in-differences (DD)
    -   Synthetic controls (SC)
    -   Instrumental variables (IV)
    -   Regression discontinuity (RD)
:::

:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Some *potential* sources of natural experiments

::: footnote
@dunning2012
:::

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "dunning.jpeg"))
```
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
-   Law changes
-   Eligibility for social programs (roll-outs)
-   Lotteries
-   Genes
-   Weather shocks (rainfall, disasters)
-   Arbitrary policy or clinical guidelines (thresholds)
-   Business / factory closures
-   Historical legacies (physical environment)
-   Seasonality
:::
:::::

## Overview {.h2center}

### [Motivation]{.gray}

<br>

### [Quasi-Experimental Designs]{.black}

<br>

### [Final Thoughts]{.gray}

## Difference-in-Differences {.center .h2center}

## Difference-in-Differences: Basic Idea

<br>

In the simplest DD setting, outcomes are observed for units in two groups and in two time periods.

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}

<br>

[Treated:]{.red}

-   Only units in one of the two groups are exposed to a treatment, in the second time period.

[Control:]{.blue}

-   Never observed to be exposed to the treatment.
:::
:::::


## Difference-in-Differences: Basic Idea

<br>

::::: columns
::: {.column width="30%"}
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "dd-dag.png"))
```
:::

::: {.column width="70%"}
The average [change]{.blue} over time (first difference) in the non-exposed (control) group is [subtracted]{.blue} (second difference) from the gain over time in the exposed (treatment) group.

<br>

Double differencing removes biases in post-period that could result from *unmeasured*::

-   fixed differences between those groups
-   secular trends affecting both groups.
:::
:::::

## Visual Intuition of DD

::: footnote
@gertler2011
:::

```{r, out.width="60%"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "gertler1.png"))
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Difference-in-Differences without Regression

DD is just differences in means! Let $\mu_{it}=E(Y_{it})$

-   $i=0$ is control group, $i=1$ is treatment.

-   $t=0$ is pre-period, $t=1$ is post-period.

-   One 'difference' estimate of causal effect is: $\mu_{11}—\mu_{10}$ (pre-post in treated)

-   Differences-in-Differences estimate of causal effect is: $(\mu_{11}-\mu_{10})-(\mu_{01}-\mu_{00})$

| Area    | Before | After | Difference (A - B) |
|:--------|-------:|------:|:------------------:|
| Treated |    135 |   100 |        -35         |
| Control |     80 |    60 |        -20         |
| T - C   |     55 |    40 |      **-15**       |

## A social epidemiology example

::: footnote
@mccormick2015
:::

```{r, out.width="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "mccormick-title.png"))
```

-   Evaluated impact of MA reform on inequalities in hospital admissions.
-   Compared MA to nearby states: NY, NJ, PA.
-   Intervention "worked": % uninsured halved (12% to 6%) from 2004-06 to 2008-09.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Evaluating pre-intervention trends

-   Adds credibility to assumption that post-intervention trends [would have been similar]{.red} in the absence of the intervention.

-   "Null" results help focus on alternative mechanisms linking disadvantage to hospital admissions.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r, out.width="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "mccormick-fig.png"))
```
:::
:::::

## Synthetic Controls {.center .h2center}

## Synthetic control methods

::: footnote
@abadie2003
:::

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
-   Inference from comparative case studies is limited if we cannot identify a control to represent the counterfactual scenario.

-   @abadie2003 pioneered the synthetic control method to examine the economic impact of terrorism in the Basque country, using other Spanish regions as control groups.

-   The synthetic control method uses a data driven approach to compare the trend of an outcome in a treated unit with the trend in a synthetic composite area (the "synthetic control").
:::
:::::

## What is a synthetic control?

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
-   A synthetic control is a weighted average of available control units that approximates the most relevant characteristics of the treated unit prior to the treatment

-   The synthetic control mimics the values of the predictors of the outcome, including pre-intervention values of the outcome, for the treated unit before the intervention occurred

-   The synthetic control represents the counterfactual scenario for a treated unit in the absence of the intervention under scrutiny

-   Intuition: A weighted combination of comparison units (the "synthetic control") provides a better comparison for the treated unit than any single comparison unit alone
:::
:::::

## 

::: footnote
@spruk2020
:::

```{r, out.width = "70%"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images",
  "dk-trans.png"))
```

## 

::: footnote
@spruk2020
:::

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
-   'Synthetic' DK mostly SWE, ITA, USA, and FIN.

-   Also declines in CVD mortality.

-   Robustness checks:

> By deliberately assigning TFA policy to wrong dates and other countries, we show the effect of the 2001 TFA policy intervention is specific to Denmark and does not appear to be driven by alternative dates
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "dk-syn-aobesity.png"))
```
:::
:::::

## Instrumental Variables {.center .h2center}



## Challenge of conventional observational study (again)

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
<br>

-   Everyone: "Educational attainment is linked to improved health outcomes."

-   But what about unmeasured confounding? Unmeasured factors such as personality traits, cognitive ability, etc. may be predictive of both education and disease.

-   Failure to measure such factors will falsely attribute their effects to education.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
<br>

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "ed-confounded-dag.png"))
```
:::
:::::

##

### Possible solution: Quasi-experiment

"Instrumental variable": predicts education but [not]{.red} associated with anything else \[mimicking random assignment\].

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "qe-educ-comp.png"))
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Non-randomized instrument creates additional issues

-   In an RCT we know the treatment assignment is not associated directly with the outcome or with other unmeasured common causes.

-   This assumption is less credible when our "instrument" is non-randomized.

```{r, out.width="70%"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "iv-bias-dag.png"))
```

##

::: footnote
@glymour2008
:::

### Non-randomized examples of IV: Policies

-   Does education affect cognitive functioning?

-   **Instrument**: changes in compulsory schooling laws \[mimicking random assignment\].

```{r, out.width="70%"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "csl-dag.png"))
```

##

::: footnote
The lower line shows the proportion of British-born adults aged 32 to 64 from the 1983 to 1998 General Household Surveys who report leaving full-time education at or before age 14 from 1935 to 1965. The upper line shows the same, but for age 15. The minimum school-leaving age in Great Britain changed in 1947 from 14 to 15 [@oreopoulos2006].
:::

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
### What does a quasi-experiment look like?

Fraction left full-time education by year aged 14 and 15 (Great Britain)
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
```{r, out.width="90%"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "uk-education.png"))
```
:::
:::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
Average schooling increases by exactly half a year between the cohorts that were age 14 in 1946 and in 1948.
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
```{r, out.width="90%"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "oreo.png"))
```
:::
:::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::: footnote
@deneve2015
:::

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
### Ex: Education and HIV
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "bor.png"))
```
:::
:::::

## Regression Discontinuity {.center .h2center}

## RD: Basic Idea

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="45%"}
<br>

-   Take advantage of arbitrary thresholds that sometimes assign treatment to individuals.

-   When an administrative or rule-based cutoff in a continuous variable (present in your data) predicts treatment assignment, being on one side or the other of this cutoff determines, or predicts, treatment received.
:::

::: {.column width="5%"}
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
<br>

-   The continuous variable is called the "assignment" or "forcing" variable.

-   Groups just on either side are the threshold considered "as good as randomly" assigned to treatment.
:::
::::::

## RD: Motivating example

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
<br>

-   Suppose we want to estimate the impact of a cash transfer program on daily food expenditure of poor households.

-   Poverty is measured by a continuous score between 0 and 100 that is used to rank households from poorest to richest.

-   Poverty is the assignment variable, $Z$, that determines eligibility for the cash transfer program.

-   The outcome of interest, daily food expenditure, is denoted by $Y$.
:::
:::::

##

::: footnote
@gertler2011
:::

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
At baseline, you might expect poorer households to spend less on food, on average, than richer ones, which might look like this
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "gertler-rd1.png"))
```
:::
:::::

##

::: footnote
@gertler2011
:::

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
Under the program's rules, only households with a poverty score, $Z$, below 50 are eligible for the cash payment
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "gertler-rd3.png"))
```
:::
:::::

##

::: footnote
@gertler2011
:::

As we approach the cutoff value from above and below, the individuals in both groups become more and more alike, on both measured and unobserved characteristics — in a small area around the threshold, the only difference is in treatment assignment

```{r, out.width="70%"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "gertler-rd6.png"))
```

## Applied example: HPV vaccine and sexual behaviors

::: footnote
@smith2015
:::

-   Does getting the HPV vaccine affect sexual behaviors?
-   Vaccine policy: predicts vaccine receipt but (**we assume**) [not]{.red} associated with anything else \[mimicking random assignment\].

```{r, out.width="70%"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "hpv-dag.png"))
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::: footnote
@smith2015
:::

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
### Does the cutoff predict treatment?

-   Girls "assigned" to HPV program by quarter of birth.

-   Pr(vaccine) jumps discontinuously at cutoff
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "smith-treatment.png"))
```
:::
:::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::: footnote
@smith2015
:::

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
### What does a credible natural experiment look like?
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "smith-t1.png"))
```
:::
:::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::: footnote
@smith2015
:::

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
### Note little impact of adjustment
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "smith-t3.png"))
```
:::
:::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Issues related to generalizability

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
-   RD estimates local average impacts around the eligibility cutoff where treated and control units are most similar and results cannot be generalized to units whose scores are further away from the cutoff (unless we assume treatment heterogeneity).

-   If the goal is to answer whether the program should exist or not, then RD is likely not the appropriate methodology.

-   However, if the question is whether the program should be cut or expanded at the margin, then it produces the local estimate of interest to inform this policy decision
:::
:::::

## Overview {.h2center}

### [Motivation]{.gray}

<br>

### [Quasi-Experimental Designs]{.gray}

<br>

### [Final Thoughts]{.black}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Be careful, and skeptical

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
-   Correlations between social factors and health are easy to find.

-   They do not necessarily reflect **causal** relationships.

-   Need to search hard for alternative explanations.

-   Important to consider the strength of evidence in considering interventions.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "feynman-fool.jpeg"))
```
:::
:::::

## Are natural experiments always more credible?

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
-   Not necessarily, but probably.

-   Key is "as-if" randomization of treatment:

    -   If this is credible, it is a much stronger **design** than most observational studies.
    -   Should eliminate self-selection in to exposure groups.

-   Allows for simple, transparent analysis of average differences between groups.

-   Allows us to rely on weaker assumptions.
:::
:::::

## Assumptions still matter!

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
-   Quasi-experimental studies are still observational.

-   Most credible if they create unconditional randomized treatment groups (e.g., lottery).

-   Credibility is continuous, not binary.

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "qe-confounded-dag.png"))
```
:::
:::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Back to basics: assumptions and costs

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
-   Major benefit of randomized evaluations are that few assumptions are needed to estimate a causal effect.

-   Necessary assumptions can often be checked.

-   Non-randomization means more assumptions, more possibility for assumptions to be violated.

-   Should lead us to spend lots of time trying to test the credibility of these assumptions.

    -   How good is "as-if random"?
    -   Are there compelling non-causal alternative explanations for the observed results?

-   Not all non-randomized designs are created equal.
:::
:::::

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
